title: Control Systems Introduction
objective: Understand feedback control, the PID controller, system response characteristics, and how to tune a controller to achieve stable, accurate robot behaviour.
beginnerExplanation:
Imagine driving a car and trying to stay in the centre of your lane. You look ahead (sense), notice you are drifting left (error), and steer right to correct (act). You do this continuously — constantly measuring the error and adjusting. This is feedback control, and it is the same principle that keeps a drone hovering at a fixed height, a robot arm following a path, and a self-driving truck maintaining its speed.

The simplest and most widely used feedback controller is the PID controller, which stands for Proportional-Integral-Derivative. It has three terms that each respond to the error signal (the difference between where you want to be and where you actually are) in a different way.

The Proportional term (P) responds to the current error. If you are 10 degrees away from the target angle, the P term pushes you back proportionally — bigger error means bigger correction. But P alone often leaves a small steady-state error and can cause oscillation.

The Integral term (I) responds to accumulated past error. If the P term is not quite strong enough to reach the target (there is a persistent small error), the I term builds up over time and provides the extra push needed to eliminate it. But too much I term causes overshoot and slow oscillation.

The Derivative term (D) responds to the rate of change of error. If you are approaching the target quickly, the D term acts as a brake, preventing overshoot. It is a damping force that smooths out the response. But D is sensitive to noise in the error signal.

Tuning a PID controller — finding the right values for the P, I, and D gains — is both a science and an art, and it is one of the most practical skills in robotics.
deeperExplanation:
Control theory provides the mathematical framework for analysing and designing feedback systems. At its core is the concept of a transfer function — a mathematical model of how a system (motor, robot arm, drone) responds to input commands. The transfer function captures the dynamics of the system: its inertia, damping, resonant frequencies, and time delays.

The behaviour of a controlled system is characterised by several key metrics. Rise time is how quickly the output reaches the target value. Overshoot is how far past the target the output goes before settling back. Settling time is how long it takes for the output to stay within a specified tolerance (typically 2% or 5%) of the target. Steady-state error is the persistent difference between the target and the actual output after all transients have died away. A well-tuned controller minimises these metrics according to the application requirements.

Stability is the most fundamental requirement. An unstable controller causes the output to oscillate with growing amplitude — the robot joint vibrates violently, the drone flips, or the vehicle swerves uncontrollably. In classical control theory, stability is analysed using the location of poles in the complex plane. Poles in the left half-plane mean stable; poles in the right half-plane mean unstable; poles on the imaginary axis mean marginally stable (sustained oscillation). The Nyquist criterion, Bode plots, and root locus are classical tools for analysing stability and designing controllers with adequate stability margins.

The Ziegler-Nichols tuning method provides a systematic starting point for PID gains. You increase the P gain until the system oscillates at a constant amplitude (the ultimate gain, Ku) and measure the oscillation period (Tu). Then you calculate P, I, and D gains from these values using standard formulas. This gives a reasonable starting point that you then refine through testing.

Beyond PID, modern robotics uses model-based control (computing the required motor torques from a dynamic model of the robot), adaptive control (adjusting controller parameters as the system changes), and optimal control (minimising a cost function over a trajectory). Model Predictive Control (MPC) has become increasingly popular — it uses a model to predict the system's future behaviour and optimises the control input over a finite horizon, re-planning at every timestep. MPC is used in advanced autonomous vehicle controllers, including some mining truck systems deployed in Australia.

In Australian mining, precise control systems are essential for autonomous operations. A 300-tonne autonomous haul truck must follow paths accurately while responding to grade changes, tyre slip on loose surfaces, and braking on steep declines. The control system must handle these disturbances while maintaining ride quality to avoid structural fatigue on the truck chassis.
keyTakeaways: Feedback control continuously measures error and adjusts to reduce it
PID control uses three terms: Proportional (current error), Integral (accumulated error), Derivative (rate of change of error)
Rise time, overshoot, settling time, and steady-state error characterise controller performance
Stability is the most fundamental requirement — an unstable controller is dangerous
Ziegler-Nichols provides a systematic starting point for PID tuning
example: Consider the altitude hold function on a DJI drone used for aerial surveying over Australian farmland. The pilot commands a hover altitude of 50 metres. The barometric altimeter and downward-facing ultrasonic sensor measure the current altitude. The PID controller computes the error (e.g. the drone is at 48 metres, so the error is +2 metres). The P term increases throttle proportionally. The I term accounts for any persistent offset caused by wind or payload weight. The D term dampens rapid altitude changes to prevent bouncing. The result is smooth, stable hovering within centimetres of the target altitude, even in gusty conditions. If the P gain is too high, the drone oscillates up and down. If too low, it responds sluggishly to altitude changes. Proper tuning — often performed automatically by the drone's flight controller firmware — balances responsiveness with stability.
commonMistakes: Cranking up the P gain too high — this causes oscillation and can damage the robot or its environment
Adding too much I gain — this causes slow, overshooting response as the integral term winds up
Ignoring the D term — derivative action provides essential damping and prevents overshoot
Tuning in simulation and expecting the same gains to work on the real robot — real systems have friction, backlash, and sensor noise that simulations often miss
Forgetting that PID is not always sufficient — some systems need feedforward, model-based control, or nonlinear control strategies
exercise: Use a free PID simulator (search for 'PID simulator online' or use the PID Tuner tool in MATLAB/Simulink if available). Set up a simple system: a motor that must reach a target angle of 90 degrees from rest. Start with only P control (I and D gains set to zero). Gradually increase P and observe: at what point does the system start oscillating? Record the ultimate gain (Ku) and oscillation period (Tu). Apply Ziegler-Nichols formulas to calculate PID gains. Test these gains and then manually refine them for a response with less than 5% overshoot and settling within 2 seconds. Document your tuning process and results.

quiz.question: What happens if the Proportional gain of a PID controller is set too high?
options: The system becomes very slow to respond
The system oscillates or becomes unstable
The system ignores the setpoint entirely
quiz.explanation: Excessive Proportional gain causes the controller to over-correct, pushing the system past the target. The resulting overshoot leads to correction in the opposite direction, creating oscillation. If the gain is high enough, the oscillations grow rather than decay, making the system unstable.

quiz.question: What is the role of the Integral term in a PID controller?
options: It prevents overshoot by acting as a brake
It eliminates steady-state error by accumulating past errors over time
It responds to sudden changes in the error signal
It sets the maximum output of the controller
quiz.explanation: The Integral term accumulates the error over time. If there is a small persistent error that the Proportional term alone cannot eliminate (for example, due to gravity or friction), the Integral term gradually builds up a correction signal that drives the steady-state error to zero. This is its primary purpose.

quiz.question: What does the Ziegler-Nichols tuning method require you to determine first?
options: The maximum motor speed and the gear ratio
The ultimate gain (Ku) and the oscillation period (Tu) at the stability boundary
The exact mathematical model of the robot arm
The desired rise time and overshoot percentage
quiz.explanation: The Ziegler-Nichols method starts by increasing the Proportional gain (with I and D set to zero) until the system oscillates at a constant amplitude. The gain at this point is the ultimate gain (Ku), and the period of oscillation is Tu. Standard formulas then calculate recommended P, I, and D gains from these two values.

perspectives.controlsEngineer: As a controls engineer, you model the plant dynamics, design the controller structure, analyse stability margins, and tune gains for optimal performance. You use tools like MATLAB/Simulink, Bode plots, and root locus diagrams.
perspectives.robotProgrammer: As a robot programmer, you tune PID gains on the actual hardware, dealing with real-world effects like friction, backlash, and sensor noise that do not appear in ideal models. You iterate between simulation and hardware testing.
perspectives.systemsEngineer: As a systems engineer, you specify the control performance requirements — how fast, how accurate, how robust — and verify that the implemented controller meets those requirements across all operating conditions.
